with (common table expressions).description: Common Table Expressions (CTEs) provide a way to write auxiliary statements for use in a larger query.

mysql.notes: Added in MySQL 8.0
mysql.syntax:
WITH cte_name AS (
  SELECT col1, col2
  FROM tbl
  WHERE …
)
SELECT *
FROM cte_name;

postgresql.notes: Well supported since PostgreSQL 8.4
postgresql.syntax:
WITH cte_name AS (
  SELECT col1, col2
  FROM tbl
  WHERE …
)
SELECT *
FROM cte_name;

sqlserver.notes: Available since SQL Server 2005
sqlserver.syntax:
WITH cte_name AS (
  SELECT col1, col2
  FROM tbl
  WHERE …
)
SELECT *
FROM cte_name;

oracle.notes: Available since Oracle 9i R2
oracle.syntax:
WITH cte_name AS (
  SELECT col1, col2
  FROM tbl
  WHERE …
)
SELECT *
FROM cte_name;

sqlite.notes: Added in SQLite 3.8.3
sqlite.syntax:
WITH cte_name AS (
  SELECT col1, col2
  FROM tbl
  WHERE …
)
SELECT *
FROM cte_name;

with (common table expressions).details: Common Table Expressions (CTEs) are named temporary result sets that exist within the scope of a single statement.

merge.description: Performs insert, update, or delete operations on a target table based on a join to a source table.
merge.syntax:
MERGE INTO target_table
USING source_table
  ON (target.pk = source.pk)
WHEN MATCHED THEN
  UPDATE SET …
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN
  INSERT (…) VALUES (…);

mysql.notes: No MERGE—use INSERT…ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE
mysql.syntax: INSERT INTO t (…) VALUES (…) ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE …;

postgresql.syntax:
MERGE INTO target_table
USING source_table
  ON (target.pk = source.pk)
WHEN MATCHED THEN
  UPDATE SET …
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN
  INSERT (…) VALUES (…);

sqlserver.notes: Available since SQL Server 2008
sqlserver.syntax:
MERGE INTO target AS T
USING source AS S
  ON T.pk = S.pk
WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET …
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT (… ) VALUES (… );

oracle.notes: Available since Oracle 9i
oracle.syntax:
MERGE INTO target T
USING source S
  ON (T.pk = S.pk)
WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET …
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT (…) VALUES (…);

sqlite.notes: No MERGE—use INSERT…ON CONFLICT
sqlite.syntax: INSERT INTO t (…) VALUES (…) ON CONFLICT(pk) DO UPDATE SET …;

merge.details: Useful for ETL and slowly changing dimensions.

json_table.description: Extracts JSON data and returns it as a relational table.

mysql.notes: Added in MySQL 8.0.4

postgresql.notes: Use jsonb_path_query
postgresql.syntax:
SELECT 
  j->>'name' AS name,
  (j->>'age')::int AS age
FROM tbl,
LATERAL jsonb_array_elements(json_col->'people') AS arr(j);

sqlserver.notes: Use OPENJSON
sqlserver.syntax:
SELECT *
FROM tbl
CROSS APPLY OPENJSON(json_col)
WITH (name NVARCHAR(50) '$.name', age INT '$.age') AS jt;

oracle.notes: Native JSON_TABLE

sqlite.notes: No JSON_TABLE—use json_each/json_extract
sqlite.syntax:
SELECT
  json_extract(value, '$.name') AS name,
  json_extract(value, '$.age')  AS age
FROM tbl, json_each(json_col);

json_table.details: Turn JSON docs into relational rows/columns for easy querying.

window functions.description: Perform calculations across a set of rows related to the current row.

mysql.notes: Added in MySQL 8.0

postgresql.notes: Supported since PG 8.4

sqlserver.notes: Since SQL Server 2005

oracle.notes: Since Oracle 8i

sqlite.notes: Added in SQLite 3.25.0

window functions.details: Includes ranking, aggregate, and value functions like LEAD and LAG.

pivot.description: Rotates rows into columns, creating cross-tab reports.

mysql.notes: No PIVOT—use conditional aggregation
mysql.syntax:
SELECT
  category,
  SUM(CASE WHEN month='Jan' THEN value END) AS Jan,
  …
FROM sales
GROUP BY category;

postgresql.notes: Use tablefunc crosstab()

sqlserver.notes: Since SQL Server 2005

oracle.notes: Since Oracle 11g

sqlite.notes: No PIVOT—use CASE/ GROUP BY
sqlite.syntax:
SELECT
  category,
  SUM(CASE WHEN month='Jan' THEN value END) AS Jan,
  …
FROM sales
GROUP BY category;

pivot.details: Shorthand for conditional aggregation—transform rows into columns.

full text search.description: Specialized indexing and querying for efficient text searches.
full text search.syntax:
-- varies by engine
SELECT *
FROM articles
WHERE MATCH(title, body) AGAINST('terms' IN BOOLEAN MODE);

mysql.notes: Engine-dependent

postgresql.notes: tsvector/tsquery
postgresql.syntax:
SELECT *
FROM articles
WHERE to_tsvector('english',title||' '||body)
  @@ plainto_tsquery('terms');

sqlserver.notes: Full-Text Service needed

oracle.notes: Oracle Text

sqlite.notes: FTS3/4/5 modules

full text search.details: Syntax and capabilities vary widely by system.

recursive cte.description: A CTE that references itself for hierarchies or graph traversals.
recursive cte.syntax:
WITH RECURSIVE cte AS (
  SELECT id, parent_id FROM tbl WHERE parent_id IS NULL
  UNION ALL
  SELECT t.id, t.parent_id
  FROM tbl t
  JOIN cte ON cte.id = t.parent_id
)
SELECT * FROM cte;

mysql.notes: RECURSIVE keyword required
mysql.syntax:
WITH RECURSIVE cte AS (…)
SELECT * FROM cte;

postgresql.notes: Standard RECURSIVE CTE
postgresql.syntax:
WITH RECURSIVE cte AS (…)
SELECT * FROM cte;

sqlserver.notes: Standard CTE recursion
sqlserver.syntax:
WITH cte AS (…)
SELECT * FROM cte;

oracle.notes: Pre-11gR2 use CONNECT BY
oracle.syntax:
WITH cte AS (…)
SELECT * FROM cte;

sqlite.notes: Supports RECURSIVE CTEs
sqlite.syntax:
WITH RECURSIVE cte AS (…)
SELECT * FROM cte;

recursive cte.details: Great for org charts, file trees, and any hierarchical data.

upsert.description: Insert rows that don't exist and update those that do in one statement.
upsert.syntax:
INSERT INTO tbl (col1,col2)
VALUES (v1,v2)
ON CONFLICT (pk) DO UPDATE
SET col1 = EXCLUDED.col1;

mysql.notes: ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE
mysql.syntax: INSERT INTO tbl (…) VALUES (…) ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE …;

postgresql.notes: ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE
postgresql.syntax: INSERT INTO tbl (…) VALUES (…) ON CONFLICT(pk) DO UPDATE SET …;

sqlserver.notes: Via MERGE
sqlserver.syntax: MERGE … WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT;

oracle.notes: Via MERGE
oracle.syntax: MERGE … WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT;

sqlite.notes: INSERT…ON CONFLICT
sqlite.syntax: INSERT INTO tbl (…) VALUES (…) ON CONFLICT(pk) DO UPDATE SET …;

select.description: Retrieve rows from one or more tables.
select.syntax:
SELECT *
FROM table_name
WHERE condition
ORDER BY column
LIMIT 10;

mysql.notes: Use LIMIT at end
mysql.syntax: SELECT * FROM tbl WHERE … ORDER BY col LIMIT 10 OFFSET 5;

postgresql.notes: LIMIT/OFFSET
postgresql.syntax: SELECT * FROM tbl WHERE … ORDER BY col LIMIT 10 OFFSET 5;

sqlserver.notes: Use TOP or OFFSET FETCH

oracle.notes: OFFSET/FETCH
oracle.syntax:
SELECT * FROM tbl WHERE … ORDER BY col
OFFSET 5 ROWS FETCH NEXT 10 ROWS ONLY;

sqlite.notes: Uses LIMIT/OFFSET
sqlite.syntax: SELECT * FROM tbl WHERE … ORDER BY col LIMIT 10 OFFSET 5;

select.details: Core retrieval statement, with dialect-specific paging syntax.

insert.description: Add new rows to a table.
insert.syntax:
INSERT INTO table_name (col1, col2)
VALUES (v1, v2);

mysql.notes: Standard
mysql.syntax: INSERT INTO tbl (c1,c2) VALUES (v1,v2);

postgresql.notes: Standard
postgresql.syntax: INSERT INTO tbl (c1,c2) VALUES (v1,v2) RETURNING id;

sqlserver.notes: Standard + OUTPUT

oracle.notes: Standard + RETURNING

sqlite.notes: Standard
sqlite.syntax: INSERT INTO tbl (c1,c2) VALUES (v1,v2);

update.description: Modify existing rows in a table.

mysql.notes: Standard
mysql.syntax: UPDATE tbl SET c1=v1 WHERE …;

postgresql.notes: Standard
postgresql.syntax: UPDATE tbl SET c1=v1 WHERE … RETURNING *;

sqlserver.notes: Standard + OUTPUT
sqlserver.syntax:
UPDATE tbl
SET c1=v1
OUTPUT inserted.*
WHERE …;

oracle.notes: Standard + RETURNING
oracle.syntax: UPDATE tbl SET c1=v1 WHERE … RETURNING * INTO …;

sqlite.notes: Standard
sqlite.syntax: UPDATE tbl SET c1=v1 WHERE …;

mysql.notes: Standard
mysql.syntax: DELETE FROM tbl WHERE …;

postgresql.notes: Standard
postgresql.syntax: DELETE FROM tbl WHERE … RETURNING *;

sqlserver.notes: Standard + OUTPUT

oracle.notes: Standard + RETURNING
oracle.syntax: DELETE FROM tbl WHERE … RETURNING * INTO …;

sqlite.notes: Standard
sqlite.syntax: DELETE FROM tbl WHERE …;

create table.description: Define a new table and its columns.

mysql.notes: Engine options available

postgresql.notes: Standard + SERIAL

sqlserver.notes: Standard + IDENTITY

oracle.notes: Standard + SEQUENCES

sqlite.notes: Standard + AUTOINCREMENT

create table.details: DDL to create a new relational table.

alter table.syntax:
ALTER TABLE table_name
ADD COLUMN new_col VARCHAR(50);

mysql.notes: Multiple ALTER per stmt

postgresql.notes: Standard

sqlserver.notes: Standard

oracle.notes: Standard

sqlite.notes: Limited: only ADD
sqlite.syntax:
ALTER TABLE tbl
ADD COLUMN nick TEXT;

drop table.description: Remove a table and all its data.

mysql.notes: CASCADE available
mysql.syntax: DROP TABLE IF EXISTS tbl;

postgresql.notes: CASCADE / RESTRICT
postgresql.syntax: DROP TABLE IF EXISTS tbl CASCADE;

sqlserver.notes: IF EXISTS in 2016+
sqlserver.syntax: DROP TABLE IF EXISTS tbl;

oracle.notes: PURGE option
oracle.syntax: DROP TABLE tbl PURGE;

sqlite.notes: IF EXISTS
sqlite.syntax: DROP TABLE IF EXISTS tbl;

set operations.description: Combine rows from multiple queries: UNION, INTERSECT, EXCEPT/MINUS.

mysql.notes: No INTERSECT/EXCEPT (emulate)

postgresql.notes: Full SET support
postgresql.syntax:
SELECT …
INTERSECT
SELECT …
EXCEPT
SELECT …;

sqlserver.notes: EXCEPT supported
sqlserver.syntax:
SELECT …
INTERSECT
SELECT …
EXCEPT
SELECT …;

oracle.notes: MINUS instead of EXCEPT
oracle.syntax:
SELECT …
INTERSECT
SELECT …
MINUS
SELECT …;

sqlite.notes: INTERSECT/EXCEPT since 3.15
sqlite.syntax:
SELECT …
INTERSECT
SELECT …
EXCEPT
SELECT …;

group by.syntax:
SELECT col, COUNT(*)
FROM tbl
GROUP BY col
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;

mysql.notes: ROLLUP extension

postgresql.notes: Standard + CUBE/ROLLUP

sqlserver.notes: Grouping sets

oracle.notes: Grouping sets/CUBE

sqlite.notes: Basic only
sqlite.syntax:
SELECT col, COUNT(*)
FROM tbl
GROUP BY col
HAVING COUNT(*)>1;

group by.details: Combine rows with same keys for aggregation.

order by.description: Sort the result set by one or more columns.

mysql.notes: NULLS LAST via ISNULL()
mysql.syntax: … ORDER BY col IS NULL, col DESC;

postgresql.notes: NULLS FIRST/LAST
postgresql.syntax: … ORDER BY col DESC NULLS LAST;

sqlserver.notes: No NULLS FIRST/LAST
sqlserver.syntax: … ORDER BY col DESC;

oracle.notes: NULLS FIRST/LAST
oracle.syntax: … ORDER BY col DESC NULLS LAST;

sqlite.notes: No NULLS FIRST/LAST
sqlite.syntax: … ORDER BY col DESC;

distinct.description: Eliminate duplicate rows in a result set.

mysql.notes: DISTINCT ON not supported
mysql.syntax: SELECT DISTINCT col FROM tbl;

postgresql.notes: Supports DISTINCT ON
postgresql.syntax: SELECT DISTINCT ON (col1) col1, col2 FROM tbl;

sqlserver.notes: Standard only
sqlserver.syntax: SELECT DISTINCT col FROM tbl;

oracle.notes: Standard only
oracle.syntax: SELECT DISTINCT col FROM tbl;

sqlite.notes: Standard only
sqlite.syntax: SELECT DISTINCT col FROM tbl;

transactions.description: Control units of work with atomicity.

mysql.notes: AUTOCOMMIT=ON by default

postgresql.notes: Standard

sqlserver.notes: BEGIN TRAN / COMMIT

oracle.notes: AUTOCOMMIT=OFF by default

sqlite.notes: AUTOCOMMIT=ON by default

transactions.details: Ensure atomic, consistent, isolated, durable operations.

string agg.description: Concatenates strings separated by a specified separator. In PostgreSQL, this is STRING_AGG; in MySQL and SQLite, use GROUP_CONCAT; in Oracle, use LISTAGG; and in SQL Server, use STRING_AGG (since 2017). Syntax and features may differ between databases.

mysql.notes: Implemented through equivalent GROUP_CONCAT function; default separator is comma

postgresql.notes: Supports ORDER BY inside aggregation

sqlserver.notes: Native support; clean syntax

oracle.notes: NOTE: Implemented through equivalent LISTAGG function; supports ordering

sqlite.notes: Alias added for compatibility with other databases; implemented as alias for GROUP_CONCAT

string agg.details: The PostgreSQL STRING_AGG() function is an aggregate function that concatenates a list of strings and places a separator between them. It does not add the separator at the end of the string.

lateral.description: Allows a subquery in the FROM clause to reference columns from tables to its left, enabling advanced row-by-row operations.
lateral.syntax:
SELECT t.id, x.val
FROM my_table t, LATERAL (SELECT val FROM other_table WHERE other_table.ref_id = t.id) x;

mysql.notes: Supported as of 8.0.14; use LATERAL keyword.

postgresql.notes: Full support for LATERAL.

sqlserver.notes: Use CROSS APPLY/OUTER APPLY instead of LATERAL.

oracle.notes: Supported as of 12c; use LATERAL keyword.

sqlite.notes: No support for LATERAL or APPLY.

lateral.details: LATERAL enables correlated subqueries in the FROM clause, making it possible to join each row to a derived table that depends on that row. In SQL Server, use CROSS APPLY or OUTER APPLY for similar functionality.

comments.description: Annotate SQL code with single-line (--) or block (/* */) comments. All major SQL engines support both styles.

mysql.notes: Both -- and /* */ supported. Special /*! version */ conditional execution syntax executes the enclosed SQL only on MySQL servers at or above the given version number.

postgresql.notes: Both -- and /* */ supported. Block comments can be nested: /* outer /* inner */ still commented */.

sqlserver.notes: Both -- and /* */ supported. No nested block comments.
sqlserver.syntax:
-- single-line
/* block */

oracle.notes: Both -- and /* */ supported. Optimizer hints use the /*+ hint */ form.

sqlite.notes: Both -- and /* */ supported. No nested block comments.
sqlite.syntax:
-- single-line
/* block */

comments.details: Single-line comments (--) and block comments (/* */) are universally supported. Notable differences: PostgreSQL allows nested block comments; MySQL supports conditional execution via /*!version ... */; Oracle uses /*+ ... */ for query optimizer hints.

join.description: Combine rows from two or more tables based on a related column. All core join types are universally supported. Search for a specific type: INNER JOIN, LEFT JOIN, RIGHT JOIN, FULL OUTER JOIN, CROSS JOIN.

mysql.notes: All standard join types supported.
mysql.syntax: SELECT a.col, b.col FROM a JOIN b ON a.id = b.a_id;

postgresql.notes: All standard join types supported.
postgresql.syntax: SELECT a.col, b.col FROM a JOIN b ON a.id = b.a_id;

sqlserver.notes: All standard join types supported.
sqlserver.syntax: SELECT a.col, b.col FROM a JOIN b ON a.id = b.a_id;

oracle.notes: All standard join types supported. Legacy (+) outer-join syntax also exists.
oracle.syntax: SELECT a.col, b.col FROM a JOIN b ON a.id = b.a_id;

sqlite.notes: All join types supported; RIGHT JOIN and FULL OUTER JOIN added in 3.39.0.
sqlite.syntax: SELECT a.col, b.col FROM a JOIN b ON a.id = b.a_id;

join.details: SQL JOINs are the primary mechanism for combining rows across tables. See specific types: INNER JOIN, LEFT JOIN, RIGHT JOIN, FULL OUTER JOIN, CROSS JOIN.

inner join.description: Returns only rows where the join condition matches in both tables.

mysql.notes: INNER keyword is optional; JOIN alone implies INNER JOIN.
mysql.syntax: SELECT a.col, b.col FROM a INNER JOIN b ON a.id = b.a_id;

postgresql.notes: INNER keyword is optional; JOIN alone implies INNER JOIN.
postgresql.syntax: SELECT a.col, b.col FROM a INNER JOIN b ON a.id = b.a_id;

sqlserver.notes: INNER keyword is optional; JOIN alone implies INNER JOIN.
sqlserver.syntax: SELECT a.col, b.col FROM a INNER JOIN b ON a.id = b.a_id;

oracle.notes: INNER keyword is optional; JOIN alone implies INNER JOIN.
oracle.syntax: SELECT a.col, b.col FROM a INNER JOIN b ON a.id = b.a_id;

sqlite.notes: INNER keyword is optional; JOIN alone implies INNER JOIN.
sqlite.syntax: SELECT a.col, b.col FROM a INNER JOIN b ON a.id = b.a_id;

inner join.details: The most common join type. Rows with no match in either table are excluded from the result.

left join.description: Returns all rows from the left table and matching rows from the right; NULLs fill unmatched right-side columns.

mysql.notes: OUTER keyword is optional (LEFT JOIN = LEFT OUTER JOIN).
mysql.syntax: SELECT a.col, b.col FROM a LEFT JOIN b ON a.id = b.a_id;

postgresql.notes: OUTER keyword is optional (LEFT JOIN = LEFT OUTER JOIN).
postgresql.syntax: SELECT a.col, b.col FROM a LEFT JOIN b ON a.id = b.a_id;

sqlserver.notes: OUTER keyword is optional (LEFT JOIN = LEFT OUTER JOIN).
sqlserver.syntax: SELECT a.col, b.col FROM a LEFT JOIN b ON a.id = b.a_id;

oracle.notes: OUTER keyword is optional. Legacy syntax: WHERE a.id = b.a_id(+)
oracle.syntax: SELECT a.col, b.col FROM a LEFT JOIN b ON a.id = b.a_id;

sqlite.notes: OUTER keyword is optional (LEFT JOIN = LEFT OUTER JOIN).
sqlite.syntax: SELECT a.col, b.col FROM a LEFT JOIN b ON a.id = b.a_id;

left join.details: Use LEFT JOIN to keep all records from the left table even when no match exists on the right.

right join.description: Returns all rows from the right table and matching rows from the left; NULLs fill unmatched left-side columns.

mysql.notes: OUTER keyword is optional.
mysql.syntax: SELECT a.col, b.col FROM a RIGHT JOIN b ON a.id = b.a_id;

postgresql.notes: OUTER keyword is optional.
postgresql.syntax: SELECT a.col, b.col FROM a RIGHT JOIN b ON a.id = b.a_id;

sqlserver.notes: OUTER keyword is optional.
sqlserver.syntax: SELECT a.col, b.col FROM a RIGHT JOIN b ON a.id = b.a_id;

oracle.notes: OUTER keyword is optional. Legacy syntax: WHERE a.id(+) = b.a_id
oracle.syntax: SELECT a.col, b.col FROM a RIGHT JOIN b ON a.id = b.a_id;

sqlite.notes: RIGHT JOIN added in SQLite 3.39.0 (July 2022). Rewrite as LEFT JOIN with tables swapped for older versions.
sqlite.syntax: SELECT a.col, b.col FROM a RIGHT JOIN b ON a.id = b.a_id;

right join.details: RIGHT JOIN is the mirror of LEFT JOIN. Since it can always be rewritten as a LEFT JOIN with tables swapped, it is less commonly used.

full outer join.description: Returns all rows from both tables; NULLs fill columns where no match exists on either side.

mysql.notes: Not supported. Emulate with LEFT JOIN UNION RIGHT JOIN.

postgresql.notes: OUTER keyword is optional.
postgresql.syntax: SELECT a.col, b.col FROM a FULL OUTER JOIN b ON a.id = b.a_id;

sqlserver.notes: OUTER keyword is optional.
sqlserver.syntax: SELECT a.col, b.col FROM a FULL OUTER JOIN b ON a.id = b.a_id;

oracle.notes: OUTER keyword is optional.
oracle.syntax: SELECT a.col, b.col FROM a FULL OUTER JOIN b ON a.id = b.a_id;

sqlite.notes: FULL OUTER JOIN added in SQLite 3.39.0 (July 2022). Emulate with UNION on older versions.
sqlite.syntax: SELECT a.col, b.col FROM a FULL OUTER JOIN b ON a.id = b.a_id;

full outer join.details: Useful for finding rows that exist in one table but not the other. MySQL has no native support and requires a UNION workaround.

cross join.description: Produces the cartesian product of two tables — every row of the left combined with every row of the right. No join condition.

mysql.notes: CROSS JOIN keyword or comma syntax (FROM a, b) both produce a cartesian product.
mysql.syntax: SELECT a.col, b.col FROM a CROSS JOIN b;

postgresql.notes: CROSS JOIN keyword or comma syntax (FROM a, b) both produce a cartesian product.
postgresql.syntax: SELECT a.col, b.col FROM a CROSS JOIN b;

sqlserver.notes: CROSS JOIN keyword or comma syntax (FROM a, b) both produce a cartesian product.
sqlserver.syntax: SELECT a.col, b.col FROM a CROSS JOIN b;

oracle.notes: CROSS JOIN keyword or comma syntax (FROM a, b) both produce a cartesian product.
oracle.syntax: SELECT a.col, b.col FROM a CROSS JOIN b;

sqlite.notes: CROSS JOIN keyword or comma syntax (FROM a, b) both produce a cartesian product.
sqlite.syntax: SELECT a.col, b.col FROM a CROSS JOIN b;

cross join.details: Rarely needed in application code but useful for generating combinations or test data. N rows × M rows = N*M result rows.

create index.description: Create an index on one or more columns to speed up queries at the cost of write overhead.
create index.syntax:
CREATE INDEX idx_name
ON table_name (column1, column2);

mysql.notes: B-tree default. FULLTEXT and SPATIAL indexes also available. Prefix indexes required for TEXT/BLOB columns.

postgresql.notes: Widest variety of index types: B-tree (default), Hash, GiST, GIN, BRIN, SP-GiST. Supports partial indexes (WHERE clause), expression indexes, and CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY (builds without locking the table).
postgresql.syntax:
CREATE INDEX idx ON tbl (col);
CREATE INDEX idx ON tbl (col) WHERE active = true;  -- partial
CREATE INDEX idx ON tbl (LOWER(col));  -- expression
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx ON tbl (col);  -- no lock

sqlserver.notes: Clustered vs. non-clustered indexes. Filtered indexes (WHERE clause) since 2008. Columnstore indexes since 2012.

oracle.notes: B-tree default. Function-based, bitmap, and domain indexes also supported.

sqlite.notes: B-tree only. Partial indexes (WHERE clause) supported since 3.8.9.
sqlite.syntax:
CREATE INDEX idx ON tbl (col);
CREATE INDEX idx ON tbl (col) WHERE active = 1;

create index.details: PostgreSQL's CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY is uniquely useful in production — it builds the index without locking the table for writes. All engines support composite and unique indexes.

create view.description: Define a named virtual table based on a SELECT query.
create view.syntax:
CREATE VIEW view_name AS
SELECT col1, col2
FROM table_name
WHERE condition;

mysql.notes: Standard views supported. No materialized views.
mysql.syntax: CREATE VIEW v AS SELECT col FROM tbl WHERE active = 1;

postgresql.notes: Standard views + MATERIALIZED VIEW (since 9.3) with REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW [CONCURRENTLY] (since 9.4).

sqlserver.notes: Standard views + indexed views (CREATE UNIQUE CLUSTERED INDEX on a view materializes it, similar to a materialized view).

oracle.notes: Standard views + MATERIALIZED VIEW (predates PostgreSQL's). Supports ON COMMIT REFRESH and ON DEMAND REFRESH.

sqlite.notes: Views are read-only. INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE through a view requires an INSTEAD OF trigger.
sqlite.syntax: CREATE VIEW v AS SELECT col FROM tbl;

create view.details: PostgreSQL and Oracle support true MATERIALIZED VIEWs (pre-computed, stored on disk). SQLite views are read-only. SQL Server achieves materialization via indexed views.

case when.description: Conditional expression returning different values based on evaluated conditions.
case when.syntax:
-- Searched CASE (most common)
SELECT CASE
  WHEN score >= 90 THEN 'A'
  WHEN score >= 80 THEN 'B'
  ELSE 'C'
END AS grade
FROM results;

-- Simple CASE
SELECT CASE status WHEN 1 THEN 'Active' WHEN 0 THEN 'Inactive' END
FROM tbl;

mysql.notes: Both simple and searched CASE. Also supports IF(condition, true_val, false_val) and IIF() as a 2-arg shorthand.
mysql.syntax:
SELECT CASE WHEN x > 0 THEN 'pos' ELSE 'neg' END FROM tbl;
SELECT IF(x > 0, 'pos', 'neg') FROM tbl;

postgresql.notes: Both simple and searched CASE. CASE can appear in SELECT, WHERE, ORDER BY, HAVING, and aggregate arguments.
postgresql.syntax: SELECT CASE WHEN x > 0 THEN 'pos' ELSE 'neg' END FROM tbl;

sqlserver.notes: Both simple and searched CASE. Also IIF(condition, true_val, false_val) since SQL Server 2012.
sqlserver.syntax:
SELECT CASE WHEN x > 0 THEN 'pos' ELSE 'neg' END FROM tbl;
SELECT IIF(x > 0, 'pos', 'neg') FROM tbl;

oracle.notes: Both simple and searched CASE. Legacy DECODE(expr, search, result, ...) function also works.
oracle.syntax:
SELECT CASE WHEN x > 0 THEN 'pos' ELSE 'neg' END FROM tbl;
SELECT DECODE(x, 1, 'one', 2, 'two', 'other') FROM tbl;

sqlite.notes: Both simple and searched CASE fully supported.
sqlite.syntax: SELECT CASE WHEN x > 0 THEN 'pos' ELSE 'neg' END FROM tbl;

case when.details: CASE is SQL-standard and works identically across all major engines. Dialect-specific shorthands (IF, IIF, DECODE) exist but are not portable.

coalesce.description: Returns the first non-NULL value from a list of expressions.

mysql.notes: Standard COALESCE. Also IFNULL(expr1, expr2) as a 2-arg shorthand.
mysql.syntax:
SELECT COALESCE(a, b, 'default') FROM tbl;
SELECT IFNULL(a, 'default') FROM tbl;

postgresql.notes: Standard COALESCE. Accepts any number of arguments.
postgresql.syntax: SELECT COALESCE(a, b, c, 'default') FROM tbl;

sqlserver.notes: Standard COALESCE. Also ISNULL(check_expr, replacement) as a 2-arg shorthand (differs from COALESCE in type coercion).
sqlserver.syntax:
SELECT COALESCE(a, b, 'default') FROM tbl;
SELECT ISNULL(a, 'default') FROM tbl;

oracle.notes: Standard COALESCE. Also NVL(expr1, expr2) for 2-arg and NVL2(expr1, not_null_val, null_val) for conditional 3-arg.

sqlite.notes: Standard COALESCE. Also IFNULL(x, y) as a 2-arg shorthand.
sqlite.syntax: SELECT COALESCE(a, b, 'default') FROM tbl;

coalesce.details: COALESCE is SQL-standard. All engines also ship proprietary 2-argument shorthands (IFNULL, ISNULL, NVL) that predate the standard.

cast.description: Convert a value from one data type to another.
cast.syntax: SELECT CAST(price AS VARCHAR(20)) FROM products;

mysql.notes: CAST(expr AS type). Also CONVERT(expr, type). Supports a limited set of target types (CHAR, SIGNED, UNSIGNED, DATE, DATETIME, DECIMAL, JSON, BINARY).
mysql.syntax:
SELECT CAST(price AS CHAR) FROM tbl;
SELECT CONVERT(price, CHAR) FROM tbl;

postgresql.notes: CAST(expr AS type). Also :: shorthand cast operator (expr::type) which is very commonly used in practice and works anywhere an expression is valid.

sqlserver.notes: CAST(expr AS type). Also CONVERT(type, expr [, style]) which adds optional style codes for date/number formatting.
sqlserver.syntax:
SELECT CAST(price AS NVARCHAR(20)) FROM tbl;
SELECT CONVERT(VARCHAR, GETDATE(), 103);  -- style 103 = dd/mm/yyyy

oracle.notes: CAST standard. TO_DATE(), TO_NUMBER(), TO_CHAR() are the traditional Oracle conversion functions for common types.

sqlite.notes: CAST(expr AS type). SQLite uses flexible type affinity; CAST influences storage affinity rather than strictly enforcing types.
sqlite.syntax:
SELECT CAST(price AS TEXT) FROM tbl;
SELECT CAST('3.14' AS REAL);

cast.details: CAST is SQL-standard. PostgreSQL's :: shorthand is unique and extremely common in practice. SQL Server's CONVERT adds format-style codes useful for date/number display.

truncate.description: Remove all rows from a table instantly, without logging individual row deletions.

mysql.notes: Resets AUTO_INCREMENT counter to its starting value. Cannot be rolled back in InnoDB without an explicit transaction.
mysql.syntax: TRUNCATE TABLE tbl;

postgresql.notes: Uniquely transactional — can be rolled back within a transaction. Supports RESTART IDENTITY / CONTINUE IDENTITY (sequence handling) and CASCADE / RESTRICT (foreign key handling).
postgresql.syntax:
TRUNCATE TABLE tbl;
TRUNCATE TABLE tbl RESTART IDENTITY CASCADE;

sqlserver.notes: Resets identity counter. Cannot truncate a table referenced by an active foreign key constraint.
sqlserver.syntax: TRUNCATE TABLE tbl;

oracle.notes: DDL operation — implicitly commits the current transaction. Cannot be rolled back.
oracle.syntax: TRUNCATE TABLE tbl;

sqlite.notes: No TRUNCATE command. Use DELETE FROM tbl (no WHERE clause). Reclaim space with VACUUM.
sqlite.syntax: DELETE FROM tbl;  -- equivalent; pair with VACUUM to reclaim space

truncate.details: Much faster than DELETE on large tables because it deallocates data pages rather than logging row-by-row. PostgreSQL is unique in making TRUNCATE fully transactional. SQLite lacks the command entirely.

like.description: Pattern matching within strings using % (any sequence of characters) and _ (exactly one character) wildcards.
like.syntax: SELECT * FROM users WHERE name LIKE 'J%';

mysql.notes: Case sensitivity depends on column collation (case-insensitive by default with utf8mb4_general_ci). REGEXP / RLIKE for full regex.
mysql.syntax:
SELECT * FROM tbl WHERE name LIKE 'J%';
SELECT * FROM tbl WHERE name REGEXP '^J[aeiou]';

postgresql.notes: LIKE is case-sensitive. ILIKE for case-insensitive matching (PostgreSQL extension). SIMILAR TO for SQL-standard regex. ~ / ~* for POSIX regex matching.
postgresql.syntax:
SELECT * FROM tbl WHERE name LIKE 'J%';    -- case-sensitive
SELECT * FROM tbl WHERE name ILIKE 'j%';   -- case-insensitive
SELECT * FROM tbl WHERE name ~ '^J[aeiou]'; -- POSIX regex

sqlserver.notes: Case sensitivity depends on column/database collation. Uniquely supports [] character class ranges in patterns: LIKE '[A-Z]%'.
sqlserver.syntax:
SELECT * FROM tbl WHERE name LIKE 'J%';
SELECT * FROM tbl WHERE name LIKE '[A-Z]%';  -- starts with any letter

oracle.notes: Case-sensitive by default. REGEXP_LIKE(col, pattern [, flags]) for full regex. ESCAPE clause for literal % or _ in patterns.
oracle.syntax:
SELECT * FROM tbl WHERE name LIKE 'J%';
SELECT * FROM tbl WHERE REGEXP_LIKE(name, '^J', 'i');  -- case-insensitive regex

sqlite.notes: LIKE is case-insensitive for ASCII letters by default (unusual). GLOB provides case-sensitive matching using * and ? instead of % and _.
sqlite.syntax:
SELECT * FROM tbl WHERE name LIKE 'j%';  -- matches 'John' AND 'john'
SELECT * FROM tbl WHERE name GLOB 'J*';  -- case-sensitive

like.details: SQLite's default case-insensitivity and PostgreSQL's case-sensitive LIKE (use ILIKE instead) are common porting gotchas. SQL Server's [] character class extension is unique among the five engines.

subquery.description: A SELECT statement nested inside another query, used in WHERE, FROM, SELECT, or HAVING clauses.

mysql.notes: All subquery types supported. Optimization historically weak in older versions — greatly improved in 8.0 with hash joins and derived condition pushdown.
mysql.syntax: SELECT * FROM t WHERE id IN (SELECT id FROM t2 WHERE active = 1);

postgresql.notes: Excellent support for all subquery types. Also supports LATERAL subqueries and efficient EXISTS/NOT EXISTS.
postgresql.syntax: SELECT * FROM t WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM t2 WHERE t2.ref = t.id);

sqlserver.notes: Full support for scalar, correlated, and derived-table subqueries.
sqlserver.syntax: SELECT * FROM t WHERE id IN (SELECT id FROM t2 WHERE active = 1);

oracle.notes: Full support. Scalar subqueries can appear in SELECT lists and many other expression contexts.
oracle.syntax: SELECT name, (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders o WHERE o.cust_id = c.id) AS cnt FROM customers c;

sqlite.notes: Full support for correlated subqueries, EXISTS, and derived tables.
sqlite.syntax: SELECT * FROM t WHERE id IN (SELECT id FROM t2 WHERE active = 1);

subquery.details: Subqueries can often be rewritten as JOINs or CTEs for readability or performance. MySQL pre-8.0 had known optimizer issues with IN subqueries that were fixed in 8.0.

trigger.description: A procedure that fires automatically in response to INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE events on a table.
trigger.syntax:
-- Syntax varies by engine; this is MySQL/SQLite style:
CREATE TRIGGER trg_name
BEFORE INSERT ON table_name
FOR EACH ROW
BEGIN
  SET NEW.created_at = NOW();
END;

mysql.notes: BEFORE/AFTER on INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE. One trigger per event/timing combination per table (before 8.0.16). Uses NEW and OLD row references.
mysql.syntax:
CREATE TRIGGER trg BEFORE INSERT ON tbl
FOR EACH ROW SET NEW.created_at = NOW();

postgresql.notes: BEFORE/AFTER/INSTEAD OF. Multiple triggers per event allowed. Trigger logic lives in a separate PL/pgSQL FUNCTION returning TRIGGER. Supports both row-level and statement-level triggers.

sqlserver.notes: AFTER and INSTEAD OF DML triggers. DDL triggers (for schema changes) since 2005. Uses inserted and deleted pseudo-tables.

oracle.notes: BEFORE/AFTER/INSTEAD OF. Row-level and statement-level. Schema-level and database-level event triggers also supported. Uses :NEW and :OLD.
oracle.syntax:
CREATE TRIGGER trg BEFORE INSERT ON tbl
FOR EACH ROW BEGIN :NEW.created_at := SYSDATE; END;

sqlite.notes: BEFORE/AFTER/INSTEAD OF (INSTEAD OF only on views). No statement-level triggers. Uses NEW and OLD row references.

trigger.details: PostgreSQL's trigger model is the most flexible, using separate reusable functions in any supported language. SQL Server triggers work with batches of rows (via inserted/deleted tables) rather than single rows.

stored procedure.description: A named, reusable block of SQL and procedural logic stored in the database.

mysql.notes: CREATE PROCEDURE with BEGIN...END. Supports IN, OUT, INOUT parameters. Called with CALL proc_name(...).

postgresql.notes: CREATE PROCEDURE added in PG 11. Key distinction from functions: procedures can COMMIT and ROLLBACK within their body. Called with CALL. Before PG 11, use CREATE FUNCTION with VOID return type as a workaround.

sqlserver.notes: CREATE PROCEDURE (or PROC). Rich T-SQL with TRY/CATCH, temp tables, and dynamic SQL. Called with EXEC or EXECUTE.

oracle.notes: CREATE PROCEDURE using PL/SQL. Called with EXEC or CALL. Rich exception handling with EXCEPTION...WHEN blocks.

sqlite.notes: No stored procedures. All logic must live in the application. Triggers can handle some reactive logic.
sqlite.syntax: -- Not supported.

stored procedure.details: PostgreSQL's CREATE PROCEDURE (PG 11+) is distinct from CREATE FUNCTION — procedures can manage transactions (COMMIT/ROLLBACK inside the body). SQLite has no server-side procedural code.

explain.description: Display the execution plan the query optimizer chose, optionally running the query to show actual statistics.
explain.syntax: EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM orders WHERE customer_id = 42;

mysql.notes: EXPLAIN shows estimated plan. EXPLAIN ANALYZE (runs the query, shows actual stats) added in 8.0.18. EXPLAIN FORMAT=JSON or FORMAT=TREE for structured output.

postgresql.notes: EXPLAIN (estimated) and EXPLAIN ANALYZE (executes and shows actual rows, loops, timing). Rich options: EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, FORMAT JSON). The most detailed output of any engine.

sqlserver.notes: SET SHOWPLAN_XML/SHOWPLAN_TEXT ON before query. SET STATISTICS IO/TIME ON for I/O and timing. Graphical execution plans in SSMS and Azure Data Studio.

oracle.notes: Two-step: EXPLAIN PLAN FOR ... stores the plan, then query PLAN_TABLE to retrieve it. DBMS_XPLAN.DISPLAY provides formatted output. AUTOTRACE in SQL*Plus is a common shortcut.
oracle.syntax:
EXPLAIN PLAN FOR SELECT * FROM tbl WHERE col = 1;
SELECT * FROM TABLE(DBMS_XPLAN.DISPLAY);

sqlite.notes: EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN shows a readable high-level plan. EXPLAIN (without QUERY PLAN) shows raw virtual machine bytecode.
sqlite.syntax:
EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN SELECT * FROM tbl WHERE col = 1;
EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM tbl WHERE col = 1;  -- bytecode

explain.details: PostgreSQL's EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) gives the richest output of any engine. Oracle's EXPLAIN workflow is uniquely two-step (write to PLAN_TABLE, then read it). SQL Server plans are best viewed graphically in SSMS.

listen/notify.description: Asynchronous publish/subscribe messaging built into the database — no external message broker needed.

mysql.notes: Not supported. Use application-layer messaging (Redis pub/sub, etc.).
mysql.syntax: -- Not supported.

postgresql.notes: LISTEN channel registers interest. NOTIFY channel [, payload] fires on transaction commit. Clients receive notifications via the connection. pg_notify(channel, payload) can be called from triggers or functions. Payload limited to ~8000 bytes. Works within a single database only.

sqlserver.notes: Not supported as SQL. Service Broker provides similar functionality but with a very different architecture and syntax.
sqlserver.syntax: -- Not supported. See Service Broker.

oracle.notes: Not supported as SQL. DBMS_ALERT and DBMS_PIPE PL/SQL packages provide async signaling but are PL/SQL-only.
oracle.syntax: -- Not supported as SQL. See DBMS_ALERT.

sqlite.notes: Not supported. SQLite is a single-process embedded database with no server process to hold pub/sub channels.
sqlite.syntax: -- Not supported.

listen/notify.details: Unique to PostgreSQL. Commonly used for cache invalidation, real-time job queue signaling, and live-update feeds — without any external infrastructure. Fires on COMMIT, not on the NOTIFY call itself.

generate_series.description: Generate a set of integer, numeric, or timestamp values as a virtual table row source.

mysql.notes: Not supported. Workaround: recursive CTE (limited to @@cte_max_recursion_depth, default 1000).

postgresql.notes: generate_series(start, stop[, step]) for integers/bigints/numerics. Timestamp support added in 8.4. Negative step works. Returns empty set if start > stop (with positive step).

sqlserver.notes: GENERATE_SERIES(start, stop[, step]) added in SQL Server 2022. Requires database compatibility level 160. Supports int, bigint, decimal, numeric. No timestamp/date support.

oracle.notes: Not supported. Workaround: CONNECT BY LEVEL for integers; recursive CTE for other types.
oracle.syntax: SELECT LEVEL AS n FROM DUAL CONNECT BY LEVEL <= 10;

sqlite.notes: Not supported. Workaround: recursive CTE.

generate_series.details: PostgreSQL's timestamp/interval support is the killer feature — filling a date spine for reporting, finding calendar gaps, or generating billing periods is trivial. SQL Server 2022 added a limited version (integers/decimals only, requires compatibility level 160).

returning.description: Return column values from rows affected by an INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE — without a separate SELECT round-trip.

mysql.notes: Not supported. Use LAST_INSERT_ID() for the auto-increment key after INSERT; no equivalent for UPDATE or DELETE.
mysql.syntax:
INSERT INTO tbl (name) VALUES ('Alice');
SELECT LAST_INSERT_ID();  -- only for the generated key

postgresql.notes: Full RETURNING on INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE. Can return any expression. Also supported in MERGE (PG 15+). Returns post-change values for UPDATE, deleted values for DELETE.

sqlserver.notes: OUTPUT clause with INSERTED.* (post-change) and DELETED.* (pre-change). Different syntax but functionally equivalent. Can redirect output into a table variable with OUTPUT ... INTO.

oracle.notes: RETURNING ... INTO :bind_var. Requires PL/SQL host/bind variables — cannot return a bare result set in plain SQL. Effectively PL/SQL-only; single-row practical for INSERT.
oracle.syntax:
-- PL/SQL context only:
INSERT INTO users (name) VALUES ('Alice')
RETURNING id INTO :new_id;
-- :new_id is a bind variable in application code

sqlite.notes: RETURNING clause added in SQLite 3.35.0 (March 2021). Syntax matches PostgreSQL.
sqlite.syntax:
INSERT INTO users (name) VALUES ('Alice') RETURNING id;
UPDATE orders SET status = 'shipped' WHERE id = 42 RETURNING *;

returning.details: MySQL's lack of RETURNING for UPDATE/DELETE is a common pain point. Oracle's implementation requires PL/SQL bind variables, making it impractical in plain SQL. SQL Server's OUTPUT clause is fully equivalent but uses different keywords.

exclusion constraints.description: A generalization of UNIQUE constraints that uses arbitrary operators — most commonly used to prevent overlapping date/time ranges or spatial objects.

mysql.notes: Not supported. Preventing overlapping ranges requires triggers or application logic.
mysql.syntax: -- Not supported.

postgresql.notes: EXCLUDE USING index_method (col WITH op, ...). Uses GiST or SP-GiST indexes. The btree_gist extension is usually needed to mix btree-comparable columns (like INT) with range/geometric operators. Can use any operator with an appropriate operator class.
postgresql.syntax:
-- Prevent room double-booking:
CREATE EXTENSION btree_gist;  -- usually required

CREATE TABLE bookings (
  room   INT,
  during TSRANGE,
  EXCLUDE USING gist (room WITH =, during WITH &&)
);

-- No two rows can share the same room AND have overlapping time ranges.

sqlserver.notes: Not supported. Only UNIQUE and CHECK constraints available; overlap prevention requires triggers.
sqlserver.syntax: -- Not supported.

oracle.notes: Not supported. Only UNIQUE and CHECK constraints available.
oracle.syntax: -- Not supported.

sqlite.notes: Not supported. Only UNIQUE and CHECK constraints available.
sqlite.syntax: -- Not supported.

exclusion constraints.details: Unique to PostgreSQL. Enables database-enforced non-overlap rules (e.g., no two reservations for the same room in the same time window) declaratively, without triggers or application checks.

range types.description: First-class data types representing a continuous range of values (integers, dates, timestamps) with built-in operators for containment, overlap, and adjacency.
range types.syntax:
-- Does a range contain a point?
SELECT '[1,10)'::int4range @> 5;  -- true

-- Do two ranges overlap?
SELECT '[1,5)'::int4range && '[3,8)'::int4range;  -- true

mysql.notes: No range types. Use two columns (start_date, end_date) and enforce constraints manually or via triggers.

postgresql.notes: Built-in types: int4range, int8range, numrange, tsrange, tstzrange, daterange. Multirange types (int4multirange, etc.) added in PG 14. Operators: @> (contains), <@ (contained by), && (overlaps), << (strictly left of), >> (strictly right of), -|- (adjacent). GiST indexable. Boundary inclusivity is explicit: [1,5) vs (1,5].

sqlserver.notes: No range types. Temporal tables (2016+) handle system-time history but are not general-purpose range types.
sqlserver.syntax: -- Not supported.

oracle.notes: No range types. INTERVAL types exist but represent durations, not ranges.
oracle.syntax: -- Not supported.

sqlite.notes: No range types. Use two columns.
sqlite.syntax: -- Not supported.

range types.details: Unique to PostgreSQL. Range types are especially powerful in combination with exclusion constraints and GiST indexes — the canonical use case is a double-booking prevention system.

filter (aggregate).description: Add a per-aggregate WHERE condition to filter the rows included in that specific aggregate, without a separate subquery.
filter (aggregate).syntax:
SELECT
  COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE status = 'active')   AS active_count,
  COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE status = 'inactive') AS inactive_count,
  AVG(salary) FILTER (WHERE dept = 'eng')    AS eng_avg_salary
FROM employees;

mysql.notes: Not supported. Use SUM(CASE WHEN cond THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) or COUNT(CASE WHEN cond THEN 1 END) as a workaround.
mysql.syntax:
SELECT
  SUM(CASE WHEN status='active'   THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS active_count,
  SUM(CASE WHEN status='inactive' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS inactive_count
FROM employees;

postgresql.notes: Full support on all aggregate functions including COUNT, SUM, AVG, array_agg, etc. Also works on window functions when the window function is itself an aggregate.
postgresql.syntax:
SELECT
  COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE status = 'active') AS active_count,
  AVG(salary) FILTER (WHERE dept = 'eng') AS eng_avg
FROM employees;

sqlserver.notes: FILTER clause added in SQL Server 2022 (compatibility level 130+). Works with standard aggregates.
sqlserver.syntax: SELECT COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE status = 'active') AS active_count FROM employees;

oracle.notes: Not supported. Use CASE expressions inside aggregates.
oracle.syntax: SELECT COUNT(CASE WHEN status='active' THEN 1 END) AS active_count FROM employees;

sqlite.notes: FILTER clause added in SQLite 3.30.0 (October 2019). Works with all built-in aggregates.
sqlite.syntax: SELECT COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE status = 'active') FROM employees;

filter (aggregate).details: Defined in the SQL standard (ISO/IEC 9075:2003). Oracle is the notable holdout. The CASE workaround is portable across all engines but significantly more verbose.

within group.description: Ordered-set aggregate functions that require a sort order to compute their result: PERCENTILE_CONT (interpolated median), PERCENTILE_DISC (exact value), and MODE (most frequent value).
within group.syntax:
SELECT
  PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY salary) AS median_salary,
  MODE() WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY dept_id)                AS most_common_dept
FROM employees;

mysql.notes: No PERCENTILE_CONT, PERCENTILE_DISC, or MODE. Median requires a workaround with subqueries or variables.
mysql.syntax:
-- Median workaround (MySQL 8.0+):
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT salary,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY salary) AS rn,
    COUNT(*) OVER () AS total
  FROM employees
)
SELECT AVG(salary) FROM ranked WHERE rn IN (FLOOR((total+1)/2), CEIL((total+1)/2));

postgresql.notes: PERCENTILE_CONT, PERCENTILE_DISC, and MODE() all supported as true group aggregates (no OVER clause required). PERCENTILE_CONT interpolates; PERCENTILE_DISC returns an actual data value.
postgresql.syntax:
SELECT
  PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY salary) AS median,
  PERCENTILE_DISC(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY salary) AS median_exact,
  MODE() WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY dept_id) AS most_common_dept
FROM employees;

sqlserver.notes: PERCENTILE_CONT and PERCENTILE_DISC supported, but ONLY as window functions — they require an OVER() clause and cannot be used as standalone group aggregates. No MODE() function.
sqlserver.syntax:
-- NOTE: requires OVER() in SQL Server:
SELECT DISTINCT
  PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY salary) OVER () AS median,
  PERCENTILE_DISC(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY salary) OVER () AS median_exact
FROM employees;

oracle.notes: PERCENTILE_CONT and PERCENTILE_DISC supported as both group aggregates and analytic functions. No MODE() — use STATS_MODE(expr) instead (added in Oracle 10g).

sqlite.notes: No ordered-set aggregates.
sqlite.syntax: -- Not supported.

within group.details: Key gotcha: SQL Server only supports these as window functions (OVER() clause required), not standalone group aggregates. PostgreSQL is the only engine with MODE(). Oracle uses STATS_MODE() as an equivalent.

row-level security.description: Attach policies to a table so different users or roles automatically see or can modify only the rows they are permitted to access.

mysql.notes: Not supported natively. Must be simulated with views or application-layer filtering.

postgresql.notes: ALTER TABLE ... ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY, then CREATE POLICY. Policies can be permissive (OR logic) or restrictive (AND logic). Superusers bypass RLS by default; use FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY to override. session variables (current_setting()) are the standard way to pass the current user identity.

sqlserver.notes: CREATE SECURITY POLICY using inline table-valued functions as filter and block predicates. More verbose than PostgreSQL. Supports FILTER predicates (hide rows) and BLOCK predicates (prevent writes).

oracle.notes: Virtual Private Database (VPD) via DBMS_RLS.ADD_POLICY(). Predates PostgreSQL RLS. Requires PL/SQL — not pure SQL DDL. Policy function returns a WHERE clause string dynamically appended to queries.

sqlite.notes: Not supported. SQLite has no multi-user access control model.
sqlite.syntax: -- Not supported.

row-level security.details: PostgreSQL's CREATE POLICY is the cleanest SQL DDL syntax. Oracle's VPD (Virtual Private Database) is the oldest implementation but requires PL/SQL. SQL Server's approach uses inline TVFs, which is powerful but verbose.

tablesample.description: Return a random sample of rows from a table efficiently, without scanning and sorting the full table.
tablesample.syntax:
SELECT * FROM tbl TABLESAMPLE BERNOULLI(10);  -- ~10% of rows, row-level
SELECT * FROM tbl TABLESAMPLE SYSTEM(10);     -- ~10% of rows, page-level (faster)

mysql.notes: Not supported. ORDER BY RAND() LIMIT n works but scans the full table and is very slow on large datasets.
mysql.syntax:
SELECT * FROM tbl ORDER BY RAND() LIMIT 1000;  -- slow
SELECT * FROM tbl WHERE id % 10 = 0 LIMIT 1000;  -- faster but deterministic/biased

postgresql.notes: Two methods: BERNOULLI (true row-level statistical sample — accurate but slower) and SYSTEM (page-level — faster but may have clustering bias). Optional REPEATABLE(seed) for reproducible results. Custom sampling methods can be added via extensions (e.g., tsm_system_rows).

sqlserver.notes: TABLESAMPLE SYSTEM(n PERCENT) or TABLESAMPLE (n ROWS). Only the SYSTEM method (page-level). Optional REPEATABLE(seed).
sqlserver.syntax:
SELECT * FROM tbl TABLESAMPLE SYSTEM(10 PERCENT);
SELECT * FROM tbl TABLESAMPLE (1000 ROWS) REPEATABLE(42);

oracle.notes: Non-standard SAMPLE clause syntax (not called TABLESAMPLE). SAMPLE(pct) is row-level (like BERNOULLI); SAMPLE BLOCK(pct) is page-level (like SYSTEM). Optional SEED(n) for reproducibility.

sqlite.notes: Not supported. Workaround: random rowid selection.

tablesample.details: PostgreSQL is the only engine offering both BERNOULLI (true row-level) and SYSTEM (page-level) methods in standard SQL. SYSTEM is faster but results can be biased toward rows that happen to share a page. Oracle uses non-standard SAMPLE syntax.

concat.description: Concatenate two or more strings into a single string. Behavior with NULL values differs significantly between engines.
concat.syntax:
SELECT CONCAT(first_name, ' ', last_name) AS full_name
FROM users;

mysql.notes: CONCAT() ignores NULL arguments (treats them as empty string). CONCAT_WS(sep, ...) skips NULLs and adds a separator between non-null values.
mysql.syntax:
SELECT CONCAT('Hello', ' ', 'World');       -- 'Hello World'
SELECT CONCAT('a', NULL, 'b');             -- 'ab' (NULL silently ignored)
SELECT CONCAT_WS(', ', 'Alice', NULL, 'Bob');  -- 'Alice, Bob'

postgresql.notes: CONCAT() ignores NULLs (like MySQL). The || operator is the standard alternative but propagates NULLs — 'a' || NULL = NULL. Use CONCAT() or COALESCE() when NULL safety matters.
postgresql.syntax:
SELECT CONCAT('Hello', ' ', 'World');       -- 'Hello World'
SELECT 'Hello' || ' ' || 'World';          -- 'Hello World'
SELECT 'a' || NULL;                        -- NULL
SELECT CONCAT('a', NULL, 'b');             -- 'ab'

sqlserver.notes: CONCAT() added in SQL Server 2012 and treats NULLs as empty strings. Before 2012, use + operator — but + propagates NULLs unless SET CONCAT_NULL_YIELDS_NULL OFF. CONCAT_WS() added in SQL Server 2017.
sqlserver.syntax:
SELECT CONCAT('Hello', ' ', 'World');       -- 'Hello World'
SELECT CONCAT('a', NULL, 'b');             -- 'ab'
SELECT 'Hello' + ' ' + 'World';           -- 'Hello World' (pre-2012)
SELECT CONCAT_WS(', ', 'Alice', NULL, 'Bob');  -- 'Alice, Bob' (2017+)

oracle.notes: CONCAT() accepts exactly two arguments. For more than two strings, use the || operator or nest CONCAT() calls. In Oracle, NULL is treated as empty string in string concatenation (|| propagates, but CONCAT ignores).
oracle.syntax:
SELECT CONCAT('Hello', ' World') FROM DUAL;          -- 'Hello World'
SELECT 'Hello' || ' ' || 'World' FROM DUAL;          -- 'Hello World'
SELECT CONCAT(CONCAT('a', 'b'), 'c') FROM DUAL;      -- 'abc'

sqlite.notes: CONCAT() added in SQLite 3.44.0 (Nov 2023). Before that, use the || operator exclusively. || propagates NULLs — use COALESCE() to guard against them.

concat.details: The biggest gotcha: the || operator propagates NULLs in PostgreSQL and SQLite (any NULL in the chain = NULL result), while CONCAT() in MySQL, SQL Server, and PostgreSQL treats NULLs as empty strings. Oracle's CONCAT() only takes two arguments. CONCAT_WS() (MySQL 2017+, SQL Server 2017+) is the cleanest option when you have a separator and want to skip NULLs automatically.

substring.description: Extract a portion of a string starting at a given position, optionally for a given length.
substring.syntax:
SELECT SUBSTRING(column, start_position, length)
FROM tbl;

mysql.notes: SUBSTRING(str, pos, len) and SUBSTR() are synonyms. Position is 1-based. If pos is negative, counts from the end of the string.
mysql.syntax:
SELECT SUBSTRING('Hello World', 7, 5);     -- 'World'
SELECT SUBSTRING('Hello World', -5);       -- 'World' (from end)
SELECT MID('Hello World', 7, 5);           -- 'World' (synonym)

postgresql.notes: SUBSTRING(str FROM pos FOR len) is the SQL-standard form. SUBSTRING(str, pos, len) also works. Supports regex: SUBSTRING(str FROM pattern) extracts the first match.
postgresql.syntax:
SELECT SUBSTRING('Hello World' FROM 7 FOR 5);   -- 'World'
SELECT SUBSTRING('Hello World', 7, 5);           -- 'World'
SELECT SUBSTRING('abc123' FROM '[0-9]+');         -- '123' (regex)

sqlserver.notes: SUBSTRING(str, start, length). 1-based position. All three arguments are required. No negative indexing from end — use LEN() arithmetic instead.

oracle.notes: SUBSTR(str, position, length). Both SUBSTR and SUBSTRING work. Position is 1-based; negative position counts from the end.
oracle.syntax:
SELECT SUBSTR('Hello World', 7, 5) FROM DUAL;   -- 'World'
SELECT SUBSTR('Hello World', -5) FROM DUAL;     -- 'World'

sqlite.notes: SUBSTR(str, start, length). 1-based. Negative start counts from the end. SUBSTRING is also accepted as a synonym.
sqlite.syntax:
SELECT SUBSTR('Hello World', 7, 5);        -- 'World'
SELECT SUBSTR('Hello World', -5);          -- 'World'

substring.details: Function name is the main portability trap: MySQL/SQL Server/PostgreSQL use SUBSTRING, Oracle prefers SUBSTR (though both work on PG and Oracle). The SQL-standard form is SUBSTRING(str FROM pos FOR len) — only PostgreSQL supports it. PostgreSQL uniquely supports regex extraction with SUBSTRING(str FROM pattern).

trim.description: Remove leading and/or trailing characters (typically whitespace) from a string.
trim.syntax:
SELECT TRIM('  hello  ');        -- 'hello'
SELECT LTRIM('  hello');         -- 'hello  '
SELECT RTRIM('hello  ');         -- '  hello'

mysql.notes: TRIM([LEADING|TRAILING|BOTH] [remstr FROM] str). Removes whitespace by default. LTRIM() and RTRIM() for one-sided trim. Can trim specific characters: TRIM(BOTH 'x' FROM 'xxhelloxx').
mysql.syntax:
SELECT TRIM('  hello  ');                  -- 'hello'
SELECT TRIM(BOTH 'x' FROM 'xxhelloxx');   -- 'hello'
SELECT LTRIM('  hello');                  -- 'hello'
SELECT RTRIM('hello  ');                  -- 'hello'

postgresql.notes: TRIM([LEADING|TRAILING|BOTH] [chars FROM] str). Also LTRIM(str [, chars]) and RTRIM(str [, chars]) which accept a set of characters to strip (not just whitespace). btrim() = TRIM(BOTH ...).
postgresql.syntax:
SELECT TRIM('  hello  ');                      -- 'hello'
SELECT TRIM(BOTH 'x' FROM 'xxhelloxx');       -- 'hello'
SELECT LTRIM('xxxhello', 'x');                -- 'hello'
SELECT BTRIM('xxhelloxx', 'x');               -- 'hello'

sqlserver.notes: LTRIM() and RTRIM() have always been available. TRIM() (both sides, whitespace) added in SQL Server 2017. TRIM() with a character set argument added in SQL Server 2022.
sqlserver.syntax:
SELECT LTRIM(RTRIM('  hello  '));           -- pre-2017 both-side trim
SELECT TRIM('  hello  ');                  -- 2017+
SELECT TRIM('x' FROM 'xxhelloxx');        -- 2022+ (character argument)

oracle.notes: TRIM([LEADING|TRAILING|BOTH] [char FROM] str). LTRIM(str [, chars]) and RTRIM(str [, chars]) also available and accept character sets.
oracle.syntax:
SELECT TRIM('  hello  ') FROM DUAL;              -- 'hello'
SELECT TRIM(BOTH 'x' FROM 'xxhelloxx') FROM DUAL; -- 'hello'
SELECT LTRIM('xxxhello', 'x') FROM DUAL;          -- 'hello'

sqlite.notes: TRIM(str [, chars]), LTRIM(str [, chars]), RTRIM(str [, chars]). The optional chars argument is a set of characters to strip, not a substring.
sqlite.syntax:
SELECT TRIM('  hello  ');                  -- 'hello'
SELECT TRIM('xxhelloxx', 'x');            -- 'hello'
SELECT LTRIM('  hello');                  -- 'hello'

trim.details: SQL Server is the notable laggard — TRIM() for both sides only arrived in 2017 (use LTRIM(RTRIM()) before that). PostgreSQL and Oracle's LTRIM/RTRIM accept a character *set* argument, not a substring — LTRIM('abcfoo', 'abc') strips any leading a, b, or c characters, not the literal substring 'abc'.

length.description: Return the number of characters (or bytes) in a string.

mysql.notes: LENGTH() returns byte length (important for multi-byte UTF-8 strings). CHAR_LENGTH() / CHARACTER_LENGTH() returns character count. For emoji/CJK text, use CHAR_LENGTH().
mysql.syntax:
SELECT LENGTH('hello');                  -- 5
SELECT LENGTH('caf\u00e9');              -- 5 bytes (UTF-8: 'é' = 2 bytes) → 6
SELECT CHAR_LENGTH('caf\u00e9');         -- 4 characters

postgresql.notes: LENGTH() returns character count (not bytes) for text/varchar. OCTET_LENGTH() returns byte length. BIT_LENGTH() returns bit count. For bytea columns, LENGTH() returns byte count.
postgresql.syntax:
SELECT LENGTH('hello');                  -- 5
SELECT LENGTH('café');                   -- 4 (characters)
SELECT OCTET_LENGTH('café');             -- 5 (bytes in UTF-8)

sqlserver.notes: LEN() returns character count, excluding trailing spaces. DATALENGTH() returns byte length (NVARCHAR = 2 bytes per char). LEN() does NOT count trailing spaces — use DATALENGTH() if that matters.
sqlserver.syntax:
SELECT LEN('hello');                     -- 5
SELECT LEN('hello   ');                  -- 5 (trailing spaces ignored)
SELECT DATALENGTH(N'hello');             -- 10 (NVARCHAR: 2 bytes/char)

oracle.notes: LENGTH() returns character count based on database character set. LENGTHB() returns byte length. LENGTHC() uses Unicode complete character count.
oracle.syntax:
SELECT LENGTH('hello') FROM DUAL;        -- 5
SELECT LENGTHB('café') FROM DUAL;        -- 5 (bytes)
SELECT LENGTH('café') FROM DUAL;         -- 4 (chars)

sqlite.notes: LENGTH() returns character count for TEXT values. For BLOB values, returns byte count. SQLite stores text as UTF-8 or UTF-16 internally.
sqlite.syntax:
SELECT LENGTH('hello');                  -- 5
SELECT LENGTH('café');                   -- 4 (characters)

length.details: The biggest trap: SQL Server uses LEN() not LENGTH() and silently ignores trailing spaces. MySQL's LENGTH() returns bytes, not characters — always use CHAR_LENGTH() for multi-byte text in MySQL. PostgreSQL and SQLite's LENGTH() correctly returns character count.

upper / lower.description: Convert a string to all uppercase or all lowercase characters.

mysql.notes: UPPER() and LOWER(). Case conversion is locale-aware based on the column's collation. UCASE() and LCASE() are deprecated synonyms.
mysql.syntax:
SELECT UPPER('hello world');   -- 'HELLO WORLD'
SELECT LOWER('HELLO WORLD');   -- 'hello world'

postgresql.notes: UPPER() and LOWER(). Locale/collation-aware. For case-insensitive comparisons, prefer ILIKE or citext extension rather than manually lowercasing.
postgresql.syntax:
SELECT UPPER('hello world');   -- 'HELLO WORLD'
SELECT LOWER('HELLO WORLD');   -- 'hello world'

sqlserver.notes: UPPER() and LOWER(). Behavior is collation-dependent. Turkish collation (tr_TR) is the classic gotcha — 'i'.UPPER() ≠ 'I' under Turkish collation rules.
sqlserver.syntax:
SELECT UPPER('hello world');   -- 'HELLO WORLD'
SELECT LOWER('HELLO WORLD');   -- 'hello world'

oracle.notes: UPPER() and LOWER(). NLS_LANGUAGE session parameter affects case conversion for locale-specific characters.
oracle.syntax:
SELECT UPPER('hello world') FROM DUAL;  -- 'HELLO WORLD'
SELECT LOWER('HELLO WORLD') FROM DUAL;  -- 'hello world'

sqlite.notes: UPPER() and LOWER() only work on ASCII characters by default. Non-ASCII characters (accented letters, etc.) are NOT case-converted without loading the ICU extension.
sqlite.syntax:
SELECT UPPER('hello');         -- 'HELLO'
SELECT UPPER('café');          -- 'CAFé' (é not converted without ICU)

upper / lower.details: SQLite is the major gotcha — UPPER/LOWER only affects ASCII by default, so accented characters are silently left alone. SQL Server's Turkish collation is a classic production bug. For case-insensitive search, avoid LOWER(col) = LOWER(input) and use engine-native features instead: ILIKE (PostgreSQL), COLLATE (SQL Server/MySQL).

replace.description: Replace all occurrences of a substring within a string with a new substring.

mysql.notes: REPLACE(str, from_str, to_str). Case-sensitive (regardless of collation). Replaces all occurrences.
mysql.syntax:
SELECT REPLACE('Hello World', 'World', 'SQL');  -- 'Hello SQL'
SELECT REPLACE('aabbcc', 'b', 'x');             -- 'aaxxcc'

postgresql.notes: REPLACE(str, from, to). Case-sensitive. For case-insensitive replace, use REGEXP_REPLACE with the 'i' flag.
postgresql.syntax:
SELECT REPLACE('Hello World', 'World', 'SQL');         -- 'Hello SQL'
SELECT REGEXP_REPLACE('Hello World', 'world', 'SQL', 'i'); -- case-insensitive

sqlserver.notes: REPLACE(str, old, new). Case sensitivity depends on the database collation. In a case-insensitive collation, REPLACE('Hello', 'hello', 'X') returns 'X'.
sqlserver.syntax:
SELECT REPLACE('Hello World', 'World', 'SQL');  -- 'Hello SQL'
SELECT REPLACE('aabbcc', 'b', 'x');             -- 'aaxxcc'

oracle.notes: REPLACE(str, search [, replacement]). If replacement is omitted or NULL, all occurrences of search are deleted. Case-sensitive.
oracle.syntax:
SELECT REPLACE('Hello World', 'World', 'SQL') FROM DUAL;  -- 'Hello SQL'
SELECT REPLACE('Hello World', 'World') FROM DUAL;          -- 'Hello ' (deleted)

sqlite.notes: REPLACE(str, old, new). Case-sensitive. Replaces all occurrences.
sqlite.syntax: SELECT REPLACE('Hello World', 'World', 'SQL');  -- 'Hello SQL'

replace.details: All five engines support REPLACE with the same three-argument signature. Case sensitivity is the key difference: MySQL is always case-sensitive, SQL Server follows collation, PostgreSQL is always case-sensitive (use REGEXP_REPLACE with 'i' flag for case-insensitive). Oracle's optional third argument (deletion mode) is unique.

current timestamp.description: Retrieve the current date and/or time from the database server. Each engine has multiple synonyms with subtle differences around timezone awareness and precision.
current timestamp.syntax: SELECT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP;  -- SQL standard, returns date+time

mysql.notes: NOW() returns datetime in session timezone. CURRENT_TIMESTAMP is a synonym. SYSDATE() returns the time at the moment of execution (differs from NOW() inside stored procs). UTC_TIMESTAMP() for UTC. CURDATE() / CURTIME() for date or time only.
mysql.syntax:
SELECT NOW();               -- '2024-03-15 14:23:01'
SELECT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP;   -- same as NOW()
SELECT UTC_TIMESTAMP();     -- UTC
SELECT CURDATE();           -- '2024-03-15'
SELECT CURTIME();           -- '14:23:01'

postgresql.notes: NOW() and CURRENT_TIMESTAMP return timestamptz (with timezone) fixed at transaction start — the same value is returned throughout a transaction. CLOCK_TIMESTAMP() returns the real current time (changes mid-transaction). CURRENT_DATE and CURRENT_TIME also available.
postgresql.syntax:
SELECT NOW();                  -- transaction start time, with tz
SELECT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP;      -- same as NOW()
SELECT CLOCK_TIMESTAMP();      -- actual wall clock (changes mid-tx)
SELECT CURRENT_DATE;           -- '2024-03-15'
SELECT CURRENT_TIME;           -- '14:23:01.123456+00'

sqlserver.notes: GETDATE() returns local server time as datetime. GETUTCDATE() for UTC. SYSDATETIME() returns higher-precision datetime2. SYSUTCDATETIME() for UTC datetime2. CURRENT_TIMESTAMP is a synonym for GETDATE().
sqlserver.syntax:
SELECT GETDATE();              -- '2024-03-15 14:23:01.123'
SELECT GETUTCDATE();           -- UTC datetime
SELECT SYSDATETIME();          -- datetime2 (higher precision)
SELECT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP;      -- synonym for GETDATE()

oracle.notes: SYSDATE returns server date/time without timezone (DATE type). SYSTIMESTAMP returns with timezone (TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE). CURRENT_DATE and CURRENT_TIMESTAMP return session timezone values.
oracle.syntax:
SELECT SYSDATE FROM DUAL;           -- server local, no timezone
SELECT SYSTIMESTAMP FROM DUAL;      -- with timezone, high precision
SELECT CURRENT_DATE FROM DUAL;      -- session timezone
SELECT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP FROM DUAL; -- session timezone with tz

sqlite.notes: No dedicated function — use datetime(), date(), or time() with 'now'. Always returns UTC. CURRENT_TIMESTAMP, CURRENT_DATE, and CURRENT_TIME are also available as SQL-standard aliases.
sqlite.syntax:
SELECT datetime('now');            -- '2024-03-15 14:23:01' (UTC)
SELECT datetime('now', 'localtime'); -- converted to local time
SELECT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP;           -- same as datetime('now')
SELECT date('now');                 -- '2024-03-15'

current timestamp.details: PostgreSQL's NOW() is fixed at transaction start — call CLOCK_TIMESTAMP() if you need it to advance mid-transaction. Oracle's SYSDATE has no timezone, SYSTIMESTAMP does — easy to mix up. SQLite always returns UTC from 'now' and requires an explicit 'localtime' modifier. SQL Server's GETDATE() precision is milliseconds; use SYSDATETIME() for microseconds.

date trunc.description: Truncate a date or timestamp to a specified unit of precision (year, month, day, hour, etc.), zeroing out all smaller units.

mysql.notes: No DATE_TRUNC(). Use DATE_FORMAT() or arithmetic with DATE() and LAST_DAY(). For truncation to a specific unit, combine YEAR/MONTH/DAY extraction and MAKEDATE/MAKETIME.

postgresql.notes: DATE_TRUNC(unit, timestamp [, timezone]). Units: microseconds, milliseconds, second, minute, hour, day, week, month, quarter, year, decade, century, millennium. Optional timezone argument added in PG 12.
postgresql.syntax:
SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', NOW());                        -- first of current month
SELECT DATE_TRUNC('hour', created_at) FROM orders;       -- truncate to hour
SELECT DATE_TRUNC('week', NOW());                        -- start of ISO week (Monday)
SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', NOW(), 'America/New_York');   -- with timezone (PG 12+)

sqlserver.notes: DATE_TRUNC(datepart, date) added in SQL Server 2022. For older versions, use DATEADD/DATEDIFF arithmetic: DATEADD(month, DATEDIFF(month, 0, col), 0).

oracle.notes: TRUNC(date, format). Format strings differ from PostgreSQL: 'MM' for month, 'DD' for day, 'HH' for hour, 'YYYY' for year. No direct equivalent for week truncation.
oracle.syntax:
SELECT TRUNC(SYSDATE, 'MM') FROM DUAL;    -- first of current month
SELECT TRUNC(SYSDATE, 'YYYY') FROM DUAL;  -- first of year
SELECT TRUNC(SYSDATE, 'DD') FROM DUAL;    -- midnight today
SELECT TRUNC(SYSDATE, 'HH') FROM DUAL;    -- current hour

sqlite.notes: No DATE_TRUNC(). Use strftime() to format and truncate dates.

date trunc.details: PostgreSQL's DATE_TRUNC is the gold standard and supports the widest range of units including quarter and decade. Oracle uses TRUNC() with different format strings. MySQL and SQLite have no native truncation — string formatting via DATE_FORMAT/strftime is the workaround. SQL Server only got DATE_TRUNC in 2022; the DATEADD/DATEDIFF pattern is how everyone was doing it before.

extract / datepart.description: Extract a specific component (year, month, day, hour, etc.) from a date or timestamp as a numeric value.
extract / datepart.syntax:
SELECT EXTRACT(YEAR FROM order_date) AS order_year
FROM orders;

mysql.notes: EXTRACT(unit FROM date) is SQL-standard and supported. Also YEAR(date), MONTH(date), DAY(date), HOUR(time), MINUTE(time), SECOND(time) as shorthand functions. DAYOFWEEK() returns 1=Sunday, WEEKDAY() returns 0=Monday.
mysql.syntax:
SELECT EXTRACT(YEAR FROM NOW());          -- 2024
SELECT YEAR(NOW()), MONTH(NOW()), DAY(NOW());
SELECT DAYOFWEEK(NOW());                  -- 1=Sun, 7=Sat
SELECT WEEK(NOW());                       -- ISO week number

postgresql.notes: EXTRACT(field FROM source) returns a float8. DATE_PART(field, source) is equivalent. Fields include: year, month, day, hour, minute, second, milliseconds, epoch, dow (0=Sun), isodow (1=Mon), week, quarter, timezone.
postgresql.syntax:
SELECT EXTRACT(YEAR FROM NOW());          -- 2024
SELECT EXTRACT(DOW FROM NOW());           -- 0=Sun, 6=Sat
SELECT EXTRACT(ISODOW FROM NOW());        -- 1=Mon, 7=Sun (ISO)
SELECT EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM NOW());         -- Unix timestamp
SELECT DATE_PART('month', NOW());         -- same as EXTRACT

sqlserver.notes: DATEPART(datepart, date) returns an integer. YEAR(date), MONTH(date), DAY(date) are shorthand. Parts include: year, quarter, month, dayofyear, day, weekday, week, hour, minute, second, millisecond, microsecond. DATEPART(weekday, date) is locale-dependent (SET DATEFIRST).
sqlserver.syntax:
SELECT DATEPART(YEAR, GETDATE());         -- 2024
SELECT DATEPART(QUARTER, GETDATE());      -- 1-4
SELECT YEAR(GETDATE()), MONTH(GETDATE()), DAY(GETDATE());
SELECT DATEPART(WEEKDAY, GETDATE());      -- locale-dependent

oracle.notes: EXTRACT(field FROM date). Fields: YEAR, MONTH, DAY, HOUR, MINUTE, SECOND, TIMEZONE_HOUR, TIMEZONE_MINUTE. TO_CHAR(date, 'format') is the traditional Oracle approach for formatting.
oracle.syntax:
SELECT EXTRACT(YEAR FROM SYSDATE) FROM DUAL;      -- 2024
SELECT EXTRACT(MONTH FROM SYSDATE) FROM DUAL;     -- 1-12
SELECT TO_CHAR(SYSDATE, 'Q') FROM DUAL;           -- quarter (1-4)
SELECT TO_CHAR(SYSDATE, 'IW') FROM DUAL;          -- ISO week number

sqlite.notes: No EXTRACT() or DATEPART(). Use strftime() with format codes: %Y=year, %m=month, %d=day, %H=hour, %M=minute, %S=second, %w=weekday (0=Sun), %W=week number.
sqlite.syntax:
SELECT CAST(strftime('%Y', 'now') AS INTEGER);   -- year
SELECT CAST(strftime('%m', 'now') AS INTEGER);   -- month
SELECT strftime('%w', 'now');                    -- 0=Sun, 6=Sat

extract / datepart.details: SQL Server uses DATEPART() instead of EXTRACT() — they're equivalent but different names. PostgreSQL's EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM ts) to get a Unix timestamp is uniquely useful. SQLite has no EXTRACT at all; strftime() with a CAST is the workaround. Week-of-year and day-of-week numbering differs between engines (ISO vs locale-based) — always test if you're using those.

having.description: Filter the results of a GROUP BY aggregation. WHERE filters individual rows before grouping; HAVING filters groups after aggregation.
having.syntax:
SELECT dept_id, COUNT(*) AS employee_count
FROM employees
GROUP BY dept_id
HAVING COUNT(*) > 10;

mysql.notes: Standard HAVING. MySQL extension: HAVING can reference column aliases defined in SELECT (e.g. HAVING total > 100 where total is a SELECT alias). This is non-standard behavior.
mysql.syntax:
SELECT dept_id, COUNT(*) AS cnt
FROM employees
GROUP BY dept_id
HAVING cnt > 10;   -- MySQL allows alias reference (non-standard)

postgresql.notes: Standard HAVING. Column aliases from SELECT cannot be referenced in HAVING (unlike MySQL) — repeat the expression or use a subquery/CTE.
postgresql.syntax:
SELECT dept_id, COUNT(*) AS cnt
FROM employees
GROUP BY dept_id
HAVING COUNT(*) > 10;   -- must repeat COUNT(*), not alias 'cnt'

sqlserver.notes: Standard HAVING. Aliases from SELECT cannot be referenced in HAVING.
sqlserver.syntax:
SELECT dept_id, COUNT(*) AS cnt
FROM employees
GROUP BY dept_id
HAVING COUNT(*) > 10;

oracle.notes: Standard HAVING. Aliases from SELECT cannot be referenced in HAVING.
oracle.syntax:
SELECT dept_id, COUNT(*) AS cnt
FROM employees
GROUP BY dept_id
HAVING COUNT(*) > 10;

sqlite.notes: Standard HAVING supported. SQLite also allows referencing SELECT aliases in HAVING, similar to MySQL.
sqlite.syntax:
SELECT dept_id, COUNT(*) AS cnt
FROM employees
GROUP BY dept_id
HAVING cnt > 10;   -- alias reference works in SQLite

having.details: The most common mistake: using HAVING instead of WHERE for row-level filters (HAVING runs after grouping and is slower). The alias-in-HAVING shortcut works in MySQL and SQLite but will fail in PostgreSQL, SQL Server, and Oracle — repeat the aggregate expression for portability.

exists.description: Test whether a subquery returns any rows. Returns TRUE if the subquery produces at least one row, FALSE otherwise. Short-circuits on the first match.

mysql.notes: EXISTS and NOT EXISTS fully supported. The subquery's SELECT list is irrelevant — SELECT 1, SELECT *, SELECT NULL all perform the same. Optimizer may convert to a join internally.

postgresql.notes: EXISTS and NOT EXISTS fully supported. Often faster than IN with large subquery result sets because it short-circuits. The planner typically converts to a semi-join or anti-join.

sqlserver.notes: EXISTS and NOT EXISTS fully supported. Prefer EXISTS over IN when the subquery can return NULLs — NOT IN with NULLs returns no rows (NULL != NULL), while NOT EXISTS handles NULLs correctly.

oracle.notes: EXISTS and NOT EXISTS fully supported. Oracle's optimizer is good at converting EXISTS to efficient join plans.

sqlite.notes: EXISTS and NOT EXISTS fully supported.

exists.details: The NULL trap with NOT IN is the critical gotcha: NOT IN returns zero rows if the subquery result contains any NULL, because SQL can't prove that the value is not equal to NULL. NOT EXISTS handles NULLs correctly and is generally the safer choice. EXISTS short-circuits at the first matching row, making it efficient even for large subqueries.

is null / is not null.description: Test whether a value is NULL (missing/unknown). NULL cannot be compared with = or != — only IS NULL and IS NOT NULL work correctly.
is null / is not null.syntax:
SELECT * FROM users WHERE phone IS NULL;
SELECT * FROM users WHERE phone IS NOT NULL;

mysql.notes: IS NULL and IS NOT NULL standard. Also has the null-safe equality operator <=> (col <=> NULL is equivalent to col IS NULL), useful in joins where NULL should equal NULL.

postgresql.notes: IS NULL and IS NOT NULL standard. IS DISTINCT FROM / IS NOT DISTINCT FROM are the null-safe equality operators: (a IS DISTINCT FROM b) is true when they differ even if one is NULL.

sqlserver.notes: IS NULL and IS NOT NULL standard. For null-safe comparison use SET ANSI_NULLS OFF (deprecated) or ISNULL()/COALESCE(). No dedicated null-safe equality operator.

oracle.notes: IS NULL and IS NOT NULL standard. Critical Oracle quirk: empty string '' is treated as NULL — there is no distinction between empty string and NULL in Oracle.

sqlite.notes: IS NULL and IS NOT NULL standard. SQLite also supports IS as a null-safe equality operator: (a IS b) is true when both are NULL or both have the same value.

is null / is not null.details: Oracle's empty-string-as-NULL is one of the most surprising production gotchas in SQL — inserting '' into an Oracle VARCHAR2 stores NULL. Null-safe equality (where NULL equals NULL) has three different syntaxes: <=> in MySQL, IS NOT DISTINCT FROM in PostgreSQL, IS in SQLite, and no clean operator in SQL Server.

row_number.description: Assign a sequential integer to each row within a result set or partition, starting at 1. Rows with equal values receive different numbers (unlike RANK).
row_number.syntax:
SELECT
  name,
  ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY dept_id ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rn
FROM employees;

mysql.notes: ROW_NUMBER() fully supported since MySQL 8.0. Not available in MySQL 5.x — use user-defined variables as a workaround.

postgresql.notes: ROW_NUMBER() fully supported. Commonly used with a CTE to filter to a specific rank (top-N per group pattern).

sqlserver.notes: ROW_NUMBER() fully supported since SQL Server 2005. The ORDER BY inside OVER() is required for ROW_NUMBER (unlike some other window functions).
sqlserver.syntax:
SELECT name, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY dept_id ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rn
FROM employees;

oracle.notes: ROW_NUMBER() fully supported. Oracle also has ROWNUM (a pseudocolumn, not a window function) which assigns numbers before ORDER BY is applied — they behave differently.
oracle.syntax:
SELECT name, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY dept_id ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rn
FROM employees;

-- ROWNUM is NOT the same:
SELECT ROWNUM, name FROM employees;  -- assigned before sorting

sqlite.notes: ROW_NUMBER() fully supported since SQLite 3.25.0 (September 2018).
sqlite.syntax:
SELECT name, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY dept_id ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rn
FROM employees;

row_number.details: The top-N per group pattern (get the highest-paid employee per department) is one of the most common uses. Oracle's ROWNUM pseudocolumn is a frequent source of confusion — it's not equivalent to ROW_NUMBER() OVER () because it assigns numbers before sorting. MySQL 5.x had no ROW_NUMBER(), which caused years of painful workarounds with session variables.

lag / lead.description: Access the value from a row before (LAG) or after (LEAD) the current row within an ordered window partition — without a self-join.
lag / lead.syntax:
SELECT
  order_date,
  amount,
  LAG(amount)  OVER (ORDER BY order_date) AS prev_amount,
  LEAD(amount) OVER (ORDER BY order_date) AS next_amount
FROM orders;

mysql.notes: LAG(expr [, offset [, default]]) and LEAD(expr [, offset [, default]]). Offset defaults to 1. Default value returned when offset goes out of bounds (instead of NULL).
mysql.syntax:
SELECT
  order_date,
  amount,
  LAG(amount, 1, 0) OVER (ORDER BY order_date) AS prev_amount
FROM orders;

postgresql.notes: LAG(expr [, offset [, default]]) and LEAD(expr [, offset [, default]]). Supports any offset and a default value for when the offset goes beyond partition bounds.
postgresql.syntax:
SELECT
  order_date,
  amount,
  LAG(amount)      OVER (ORDER BY order_date)          AS prev_amount,
  LAG(amount, 2)   OVER (ORDER BY order_date)          AS two_ago,
  LEAD(amount, 1, 0) OVER (PARTITION BY cust_id ORDER BY order_date) AS next_amount
FROM orders;

sqlserver.notes: LAG(expr [, offset [, default]]) and LEAD(expr [, offset [, default]]). Added in SQL Server 2012. Offset must be a non-negative integer constant.
sqlserver.syntax:
SELECT
  order_date,
  amount,
  LAG(amount, 1, 0)  OVER (ORDER BY order_date) AS prev_amount,
  LEAD(amount, 1, 0) OVER (ORDER BY order_date) AS next_amount
FROM orders;

oracle.notes: LAG(expr [, offset [, default]]) and LEAD(expr [, offset [, default]]). One of the earliest implementations — Oracle has had these since 8i.
oracle.syntax:
SELECT
  order_date,
  amount,
  LAG(amount, 1, 0)  OVER (ORDER BY order_date) AS prev_amount,
  LEAD(amount, 1, 0) OVER (ORDER BY order_date) AS next_amount
FROM orders;

sqlite.notes: LAG() and LEAD() fully supported since SQLite 3.25.0 (September 2018).
sqlite.syntax:
SELECT
  order_date,
  amount,
  LAG(amount)  OVER (ORDER BY order_date) AS prev_amount,
  LEAD(amount) OVER (ORDER BY order_date) AS next_amount
FROM orders;

lag / lead.details: LAG/LEAD replaced the classic self-join pattern (join a table to itself on id-1). The optional default argument (third parameter) controls what's returned at partition boundaries — without it you get NULL, which can break calculations. SQL Server didn't add these until 2012; Oracle had them since 8i.

charindex / instr / position.description: Search for a substring within a string and return the position of the first occurrence. Returns 0 or NULL if not found, depending on the engine.
charindex / instr / position.syntax:
-- Standard SQL:
POSITION('needle' IN haystack_col)

-- Common alternatives:
CHARINDEX('needle', haystack_col)
INSTR(haystack_col, 'needle')

mysql.notes: INSTR(str, substr) and LOCATE(substr, str [, start]) are the native functions. POSITION(substr IN str) also works as SQL-standard syntax. Returns 0 if not found (not NULL).
mysql.syntax:
SELECT INSTR('hello world', 'world');       -- 7
SELECT LOCATE('world', 'hello world');     -- 7
SELECT LOCATE('world', 'hello world', 8);  -- 0 (start after pos 8)
SELECT POSITION('world' IN 'hello world'); -- 7

postgresql.notes: POSITION(substr IN str) is the standard form. STRPOS(str, substr) is a PostgreSQL-specific alias with reversed argument order. Returns 0 if not found. No CHARINDEX or INSTR.
postgresql.syntax:
SELECT POSITION('world' IN 'hello world');  -- 7
SELECT STRPOS('hello world', 'world');      -- 7

sqlserver.notes: CHARINDEX(needle, haystack [, start]) is the primary function. Returns 0 if not found. PATINDEX('%pattern%', str) supports LIKE-style wildcards. No INSTR or POSITION.
sqlserver.syntax:
SELECT CHARINDEX('world', 'hello world');      -- 7
SELECT CHARINDEX('world', 'hello world', 8);   -- 0
SELECT PATINDEX('%wor%', 'hello world');        -- 7
